coredata.dc:description: The current invention pertains to the expeditious simple synthesis of electrode materials that improve the storage capacity of supercapacitors (SCs). Sulfur and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots (SN-GQDs) are synthesized using a microwave-assisted hydrothermal (MAH) process at low pressure and with a short reaction time. The utilization of SN-GQDs in conjunction with Polyaniline (PANI) has the potential to enhance the supercapacitor's energy and power density, owing to their notable specific capacitance. Implementing SN-GQDs material as an SCs electrode, exhibiting an outstanding specific capacitance of 1040 F/g at an applied current density of 0.5 A g−1. Furthermore, a composite of SN-GQDs/PANI is synthesized and the electrochemical performance is compared with the as-synthesized PANI. The symmetrical SCs are fabricated using SN-GQDs/PANI composite, and PANI. At a current density of 0.5 A g−1 SN-GQDs/PANI composite-based SC displays a superior energy density of 44.25 Wh/kg at a power density of 1.227 kW/kg. This is high in comparison to PANI-based SC which shows an energy density of 18.71 Wh/kg at 0.8 kW/kg power density at the same current density. The SC created using SN-GQDs/PANI composite exhibits superior properties and is a promising material for SC applications.

head.ce:title: Facile and rapid method to synthesis sulfur and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots as an electrode material with excellent specific capacitance for supercapacitors application

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The current invention pertains to the expeditious simple synthesis of electrode materials that improve the storage capacity of supercapacitors (SCs). Sulfur and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots (SN-GQDs) are synthesized using a microwave-assisted hydrothermal (MAH) process at low pressure and with a short reaction time. The utilization of SN-GQDs in conjunction with Polyaniline (PANI) has the potential to enhance the supercapacitor's energy and power density, owing to their notable specific capacitance. Implementing SN-GQDs material as an SCs electrode, exhibiting an outstanding specific capacitance of 1040 F/g at an applied current density of 0.5 A g−1. Furthermore, a composite of SN-GQDs/PANI is synthesized and the electrochemical performance is compared with the as-synthesized PANI. The symmetrical SCs are fabricated using SN-GQDs/PANI composite, and PANI. At a current density of 0.5 A g−1 SN-GQDs/PANI composite-based SC displays a superior energy density of 44.25 Wh/kg at a power density of 1.227 kW/kg. This is high in comparison to PANI-based SC which shows an energy density of 18.71 Wh/kg at 0.8 kW/kg power density at the same current density. The SC created using SN-GQDs/PANI composite exhibits superior properties and is a promising material for SC applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Storage technologies are crucial components of the transformation of the energy system because they not only help store electricity for later use but also stabilize the flow of electricity, which is important when dealing with intermittent power sources. Supercapacitors or ultracapacitors may be candidates for the upcoming generation of energy storage devices that are effective, safe, and cyclically long-lasting energy storage technologies [1–4]. Supercapacitors can be used in various applications, including power hybrid electric cars and other electronic systems that depend on electricity to function [5,6]. Supercapacitors are divided into three groups based on the mechanism of energy storage. The first one is the electrochemical double layer capacitor (EDLC) which follows the mechanism of electrostatically ion adsorption and desorption between the electrode and electrolytes. The second one is the pseudo capacitors in which reversible faradaic redox reactions are involved between electrolyte ions and electroactive materials. The third case is the hybrid consisting of both mechanisms [7,8].

However, the key to energy storage for supercapacitors at the moment lies in the electrode materials, and scientists are eager to discover new electrode materials to make better energy storage devices. Large specific surface area materials provide more interfaces for ion adsorption and desorption during electrochemical activity, increasing the contact area between the active material and the electrolyte and accelerating ion diffusion, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are one of those materials [9–13]. It is also non-toxic and has biologically inert properties, making it environmentally benign [3,14,15]. The challenge in the synthesis of graphene quantum dots is to control the size of GQDs below 10 nm. Few reports used the conventional hydrothermal method [16,17] but the particle size distribution is from 2 to 16 nm. Some reports used other than the hydrothermal method but grew quantum dots even more than these ranges mentioned above [18–20]. Therefore, the reason why their value of specific capacitance may be less. In our work, we have taken the challenges to synthesize GQDs in a controlled way to get size distribution below 10 nm. The lower-size GQDs will have more surface area than the large-size GQDs.

However, doping is an effective method for enhancing the properties of graphene quantum dots because it increases the electrochemically active sites at the edges, resulting in better pseudocapacitive behavior [21]. The sulfur and nitrogen not only improve the electrical conductivity but also act as an additional trap center for charge carriers, increasing storage capacity [22]. PANI stands out among the various electrode materials for SCs due to its high theoretical pseudo capacitance, superior electronic conductivity, reversible doping/de-doping, low cost, ease of production, and great stability [23–26].

GQDs have been synthesized using various methods, including top-down and bottom-up approaches [27,28]. In the bottom-up approach, we use chemical reactions between atoms, ions, and molecules to synthesize quantum dots using precursors however the top-down approach involves cutting bulk materials into quantum dots [27]. In the traditional hydrothermal method, a reaction takes tens of hours to produce the same materials, whereas the MAH method frequently exhibits order-of-magnitude improvements in materials synthesis [29,30]. When compared to the conventional hydrothermal approach, the MAH method can greatly reduce reaction times, lesser power consumption, and better yield [31,32]. We anticipate that research in the expanding field of rapid MAH synthesis of quantum dot materials will continue to grow in the years to come as synthesis can be done in a controlled manner to achieve uniform particle size.

In our work, we have synthesized SN-GQDs using microwave microwave-assisted hydrothermal (MAH) method in a very short time. The synthesized GQDs are grown not only very fast but in a controlled manner which gives a narrow size distribution of GQDs. The precursor used is ammonium sulfate as a source of sulfur and nitrogen, which are different from what other researchers have used till now as far as we know. The results show excellent electrochemical values and it enhances the energy density of the supercapacitors when used in conjunction with PANI.

Glucosamine hydrochloride, ammonium sulfate, ammonium persulfate (APS), aniline monomer, 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), and KOH were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. HCl and H2SO4 were obtained from Isochem laboratories. Every chemical was of analytical grade, and it was used exactly as it was received. Moreover, unless specified otherwise, all of the solutions were prepared using deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm).

SN-GQDs were synthesized through the utilization of Glucosamine hydrochloride and ammonium sulfate as precursors in a microwave synthesis reactor (Monowave 200, Anton Paar, Austria), employing an MAH method. Ammonium sulfate was used as a sulfur and nitrogen source in the synthesis of SN-GQDs, with a glucosamine-hydrochloride to ammonium sulfate ratio of 1:2. Inside the reactor, a mixture of 0.02 M aqueous solution of glucosamine hydrochloride and 0.04 M aqueous solution of ammonium sulfate was processed. The reaction took place at a temperature of 160 °C and was maintained under a pressure of 5.4 bar for 10 min. The desired size of the synthesized S, N-GQDs is achieved through a series of ultracentrifugation steps at a speed of 20,000 rpm for 20 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PANI and SN-GQDs/PANI composite
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesize PANI [33], 1.0 g of aniline monomer was dispersed in 30 mL of a 1 M HCl solution. This dispersion underwent stirring for 30 min. Following that, a solution containing 2.28 g of ammonium persulfate ((NH4)2S2O8) dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water was introduced into the systems mentioned above. The process of polymerization was carried out for 24 h at ambient temperature. The product underwent ultracentrifugation, a meticulous washing procedure consisting of three cycles of centrifugation first using deionized water and then with the ethanol. Finally, the product immersed in ethanol was dried for 24 h at 60 °C.

To synthesize the SN-GQDs/PANI composite, the same procedure was followed in which 1.0 g of aniline monomer was dispersed in 30 mL of a 1 M HCl solution and dispersion underwent stirring for 30 min. Subsequently, 5 mL of SN-GQDs was added to the solutions. Following that, a solution containing 2.28 g of ammonium persulfate ((NH4)2S2O8) dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water was introduced into the system mentioned above and then the same cleaning procedure was performed after the synthesis. This procedure was carried out to facilitate the subsequent characterization and fabrication of supercapacitors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of electrode and the device
ce:section.ce:para: The working electrode for the three-electrode measurement was prepared by combining a 90 % solution of SN-GQDs with 10 % acetylene black in a small vial. Subsequently, 10 μL of Nafion was added as a binder, and the mixture was sonicated in water for 2 h. The resulting solution was then used to coat both sides of a nickel foam by immersing it in the solution. The coated electrode was later dried overnight in a vacuum oven at a temperature of 65 °C.

The measurement of symmetric supercapacitors was carried out using a 0.5 M H2SO4 aqueous electrolyte solution. The fabrication process of the PANI-based and SN-GQDs/PANI composite-based electrode materials for the symmetric supercapacitors involved the combination of 80 % active materials, specifically the PANI and SN-GQDs/PANI composite and the remaining 10 % PVDF (polyvinylidene fluoride) which is used as a binder and 10 % Carbon black. The electrode materials were dispersed by adding a small amount (approximately 1 mL) of NMP (N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone) solution and subjecting it to sonication. Ultimately, the solution was applied consistently onto a commercially accessible flexible carbon cloth, with each cloth measuring 1 cm × 1 cm and having a total coating density of 1 mg/cm2. The resulting coated carbon fabric was subsequently subjected to a drying process at a temperature of 60 °C. Before utilization, the carbon cloth underwent a preliminary cleansing process involving the use of acetone and deionized water. Following that, the already treated carbon cloth was positioned between two current collectors. The Whatman filter paper of 5 μm was drenched with the electrolyte solution and employed as a separator.

The SN-GQDs imaging was done using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) imaging (Tecnai™ F30, ThermoFisher®, USA), and the height profile of the SN-GQDs was analyzed using an atomic force microscope (AFM) (Flex-Axiom AFM, Nanosurf, Switzerland). For AFM sample preparation, SN-GQDs were spin-coated on the mica sheet. In addition to the imaging, the elemental mapping was done by using Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy in HR-TEM to confirm the presence of sulfur and nitrogen in the SN-GQDs. Also, TEM (2100, JEOL, Japan) was used to calculate the particle size and polydisperse of SN-GQDs. FESEM (7610Plus, JEOL, Japan) imaging and EDS were used to find out the morphology of the PANI and the SN-GQDs/PANI composite along with the elemental composition. The elemental binding energy of the bonds in SN-GQDs was confirmed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Scienta Omicron, Sweden). The structure of SN-GQDs, PANI, and SN-GQDs/PANI composite were characterized using the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) (Spectrum 2 FT-IR Spectrometer, PerkinElmer, Singapore) spectra recorded in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. Raman spectra of SN-GQDs, PANI, and SN-GQDs/PANI composite were obtained by a Raman microscope (Compact Raman Spectrometer, Renishaw, UK). The crystal structure of PANI and SN-GQDs/PANI composite were elucidated using the X-ray diffractometer (Empyrean 3rd Gen, Malvern PANalytical, Netherlands) using Cu-Kα radiation at 2θ ranging between 10 and 50°. N2 adsorption-desorption was performed using a BET surface area analyzer (Autosorb IQ-XR-XR, Anton Paar, Austria). Electrical conductivity measurement was performed using four probe methods.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charging-discharging (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were conducted in a three-electrode system using a 6 M KOH aqueous solution in (Origaflex, Orygalys, UK) electrochemical workstation. The Pt electrode, calomel electrode, and prepared electrode were employed as the counter electrode, reference electrode, and working electrode, respectively. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out at an AC potential of 5 mV over a frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 1 MHz.

For the three-electrode system, the specific capacitance of the electrode was calculated by the following equation [34,35]:
(1)
C s

2 I ∫ Vdt

V f 2
,
where C
s is the specific capacitance (F/g), I reflects the constant current (A), m is the loading mass of the active materials on the electrodes (g), V is the voltage range (V), and the term ∫ Vdt represents the area under the experimental curve.

The electrochemical behavior of supercapacitors (SCs) was tested by using two electrodes in 0.5 M H2SO4 solutions under a voltage range of 0.0–1.0 V. PANI SCs with H2SO4 electrolyte shows a better EDLC effect [36]. The following formula was used to get specific capacitance (C
s ) of the SCs using galvanometric charge-discharge curves [37]:
(2)
C s
= 4 I Δ t / m Δ V ,
here, I is the constant current (A), m is the total mass of both electrodes (g), ∆t represents the discharge time (s), and ∆V represents the voltage drop during the discharging process (V). The multiplier of 4 is used in the equation to convert the mass of the two electrodes and the specific capacitance of the cell to those of a single electrode [13].

The energy density (E, Wh kg−1) and power density (P, Wkg−1) were calculated using the following formula [12]:
(3)
E =
C s
V 2
/ 2 × 3.6 ,
(4)
P = 3600 E / Δ t ,
where Δt (s), C
s (F/g), and V (V) represented discharge time, specific capacitance, and voltage window of the device, respectively.

Fig. 1(a–b) represents an image of SN-GQDs obtained using a HRTEM. Their high crystallization has been validated through the analysis of the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (shown in inset Fig. 1a). Furthermore, the interplanar distance obtained from the SAED pattern and inverse fast Fourier transform (FFT) analysis exhibits a proximity of around 0.31 nm and 0.32 nm, respectively. These values match the crystallographic plane denoted as (002) in graphene [38]. The topography was examined using AFM, which revealed an evenly sized distribution and height profile. The mean height of SN-GQDs is approximately 3.13 nm ± 1 nm, as depicted in Fig. 1(c–d). The confirmation of the presence of sulfur and nitrogen was also obtained through elemental mapping of the image in HRTEM as depicted in Fig. 2 . Also, the size of SN-GQDs was confirmed with the polydisperse data which is 4.1 ± 0.7 nm can be seen from Fig. 1e.

Fig. S1 (Supporting Information) presents the FESEM images of the PANI and SN-GQDs/PANI composites. As shown in the figure PANI samples show irregular shapes with agglomeration and less porous nature. In the SN-GQDs/PANI composite, we observe different morphology with lesser agglomeration. However, the FESEM (EDS) provides the amount of nitrogen and sulfur available in both the samples which will provide evidence for more active sites and better electrical conductivity. In PANI, the atomic percentage of nitrogen and carbon was 16.2 % and 83.8 %. Whereas the atomic percentage of sulfur in SN-GQDs/PANI composite was 8.7 % along with 12.2 % nitrogen and 79.1 % carbon. Therefore, the addition of SN-GQDs can indeed enhance the electrochemical properties of PANI.

To ascertain the chemical states of the as-prepared SN-GQDs, XPS analysis was conducted, as depicted in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3a displays the complete scan XPS spectrum of SN-GQDs. The spectrum reveals the presence of four distinct peaks located at 531, 400, 284, and 167 eV, which are attributed to the O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, and S 2p electron orbitals, respectively. The deconvoluted peaks of the C 1s spectra (see Fig. 3b) correspond to the sp2 hybridized CC carbon at binding energy 283.59 eV, the CH carbon at 284.14 eV, the CN/CO bond at 285.20 eV, and the CS bond of SN-GQDs at 287.49 eV [39]. The N 1s XPS spectrum depicted in Fig. 3c has three distinct peaks at 399.08, 399.64, and 402.32 eV, which can be ascribed to pyrrolic N, pyridinic N, and graphitic N, respectively [40].

The high-resolution S 2p XPS spectrum peaks depicted in Fig. 3d are situated at 167.56, 168.48, and 169.56 eV, which correspond to the chemical species (CSO), (C-SO4), and S 2p3/2, respectively [41,42]. Fig. 3e illustrates the excellent resolution of the O 1 s spectrum, whereby prominent peaks at 530.96 and 531.92 eV are observed, corresponding to the CO and CO/COH functional groups, respectively [39]. The addition of S and N atoms to the graphene layers increases the number of electrons available to the graphene's π-conjugated system, improving the sample's conductivity [43]. Furthermore, the addition of electrochemically active sites and pseudo-capacitance to the graphene structure due to the presence of sulfur (S) and nitrogen (N) enhances the capacitance properties.

Fig. 4a displays the Raman spectra of SN-GQDs, revealing the presence of the D and G bands. These bands signify the existence of a disordered carbon structure and the sp2 hybridization of carbon atoms in the graphitic carbon [44]. In the FTIR spectra of SN-GQDs (as shown in Fig. 4b), the observed bands at wavenumbers 2065 cm−1, 1402 cm−1, and 1130 cm−1 correspond to the vibrational absorption bands associated with the chemical bonds CN, CN, and CS, respectively [45,46]. The absorption peaks observed at 748 cm−1 and about 2900 cm−1 are indicative of the stretching vibrations associated with the CS and CH bonds, respectively [45,47]. The absorption peaks seen at wavenumbers 3435 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 correspond to the vibrational modes of the OH stretching in water molecules present in the surrounding atmosphere and the stretching vibration of the CC bond inside the graphite structure, respectively [48].

Fig. 5a displays the XRD patterns of both the polyaniline (PANI) and the composite SN-GQDs/PANI. Three diffraction peaks were observed in the pure PANI sample at angles of 15.14°, 20.38°, and 25.4°, which attributed to the crystal planes (011), (020), and (200) of PANI [49]. These peaks show the periodicity parallel to and perpendicular to the polymer chains, respectively, and are representative of the parallel repeat units of PANI [50,51]. When incorporating SN-GQDs into the PANI matrix, the broad peaks originating from the graphene quantum dots, occurring at around 25–28°, coincide with the peak of PANI, resulting in enhanced peaks in the composite. The FWHM of the (200) plane of PANI and SN-GQDs/PANI composite is 2.559° and 2.438°, respectively. This confirms that composite material becomes more crystalline due to the presence of SN-GQDs.

In Fig. 5b, the Raman spectra of PANI and the composite are depicted. The Raman bands corresponding to PANI are seen at wavenumbers of 1166 cm−1, 1244 cm−1, 1339 cm−1, 1474 cm−1, and 1590 cm−1 [52]. The spectral peak observed at a wavenumber of 1166 cm−1 is ascribed to the in-plane bending vibration of the CH bonds inside the 1, 4-disubstituted benzenoid/quinoid rings of PANI [53]. The CN stretching mode with low intensity is detected at a wavenumber of 1244 cm−1. The spectral peak observed at a wavenumber of 1339 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the CN + bond in the semiquinone radical cation, as well as the delocalized polaronic structure of PANI [39].

The observed spectral peaks at wavenumbers of 1474 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of the HCCH and CC bonds in PANI. The band intensity of SN-GQDs/PANI is seen to be sharp and clear. These sharper peaks indicate that the composites become more crystalline compared to PANI. Additionally, the peak at 1251 cm−1 shows a noticeable shift relative to PANI [54]. These observations provide evidence for site-selective interaction within the SN-GQDs/PANI composite. Hence, the above evidence confirms the π–π and electrostatic interactions between SN-GQDs and PANI. Therefore, it may enhance the charge transfer properties of composites.

Fig. 5c depicts the FTIR spectra of PANI and the SN-GQDs/PANI composite. In this spectrum, the primary peaks of PANI are observed at 790 cm−1 and 1105 cm−1, which correspond to the CH stretching vibration of the 1,4-disubstituted benzene ring's out-of-plane deformation vibration and the delocalized electron formation of the -NH
+ stretching vibration in the polymer backbone, respectively [55,56]. The peak corresponding to the CO bond, observed at 2346 cm−1 for PANI samples might be arising from surface contamination due to the presence of atmospheric carbon dioxide [57]. The observed peaks at wavenumbers 1232 cm−1 and 1298 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of the CN and CN bonds, respectively, of the secondary aromatic amine present in the polymer chain [58,59].

The peak observed at a wavenumber of 1637 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of the CC bond of both the benzenoid and quinoid ring structures [55,60]. The wavenumbers 2926 cm−1 and 3450 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibrations of the CH bond and the OH bond, respectively. In contrast, it is observed that the SN-GQDs/PANI composite exhibits an increase in the intensity of the vibrational modes at 3450 cm−1, 1637 cm−1, and 1105 cm−1.

The FWHM of the 3450 cm−1, 1637 cm−1, and 1105 cm−1 peaks of the SN-GQDs/PANI composite is 333 cm−1, 82 cm−1, and 90 cm−1 and PANI is 361 cm−1, 128 cm−1, and 150 cm−1. This indicates that by addition of SN-GQDs in the PANI matrix makes the composite more crystalline. Additionally, a shift in the peak position is evident, specifically from 1105 cm−1 to 1121 cm−1 [61,62]. This peak shift confirms the interaction between SN-GQDs and PANI in the nanocomposites. Also, the observed shift in peaks provides evidence for the existence of electrostatic interaction between SN-GQDs, and PANI, hence validating the incorporation of SN-GQDs within the PANI matrix.

Specific surface area analysis of PANI and SN-GQD/PANI composites are characterized by nitrogen adsorption/desorption tests at 77 K and the isotherm plot is given in Fig. S2 (Supporting Information). Isotherm curves of both materials follow the type III isotherm with a hysteresis loop of type H3 [22]. The BET surface area of the PANI and SN-GQDs/PANI composite are 39 m2/g and 38 m2/g. There is no significant difference in the surface area observed because the very small wt% of QDs were added.

Fig. 6 illustrates the three-electrode electrochemical test of SN-GQDs as an active material. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) results are shown in Fig. 6a of the bare nickel (Ni) foam along with the pristine GQDs and SN-GQDs. Ni foam exhibits minor redox peaks characterized by a limited integrated area and specific capacitance. Whereas SN-GQDs on the Nickel foam show the enhanced integrated area. Also, the GQDs peak shows a different redox peak in comparison with SN-GQDs. The integrated area is maximum in the case of SN-GQDs because of the sulfur and nitrogen doping in the pristine GQDs. This shows the increase in the specific capacitance and the energy storage capability of the materials. Although the specific capacitance of Ni foam is relatively low compared to active materials, it serves as an effective current collector and facilitates ion movement [63].

Fig. 6b depicts the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curve of the SN-GQDs electrode at various scan rates, spanning from 5 to 200 mV/s. The graph demonstrates that SN-GQDs display pseudo capacitance behavior, characterized by a pair of redox peaks, throughout a potential window of 100–500 mV in a 6 M KOH aqueous electrolyte. The introduction of sulfur (S) and nitrogen (N) doping has been found to have a positive effect on pseudo-capacitance and ion migration, leading to an overall rise in specific capacitance [64,65].

The specific capacitance was determined through the use of the GCD technique, as depicted in Fig. 6c. Since the discharging curve shows non-linear behavior, the specific capacitance was calculated using Eq. 1. The specific capacitance values obtained were 1040, 926, 870, 831, and 823 F/g for current densities of 0.5, 1, 2, 3, and 4 A g−1, respectively. This value surpasses the reported specific capacitance of graphene quantum dots-based electrodes reported in earlier studies as can be seen in Table 1 . These findings suggest that SN-GQDs have sustainable performance across a wide range of currents. The observed reduction in specific capacitance as current density increases indicates the deterioration in electrochemical performance under high current density as shown in Fig. 6d. This can be attributed to the limited accessibility of ions to diffuse into the pores of electrode materials by the electrolytic ions [66,67].

For further insight into the exceptional electrochemical performance exhibited by the SN-GQDs, an Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) analysis was conducted. Fig. 6e illustrates the Nyquist plot, where the SN-GQDs demonstrate a distinct semi-circular shape in the high-frequency region, indicating a reduced charge transfer resistance [68,69]. This reduction in resistance has a direct influence on the diffusion rates of ions, leading to enhanced electrode conductivity. Additionally, a linear trend is observed in the low-frequency region. The determined values for the equivalent series resistance (Rs) and charge transfer resistance (Rct) are 0.283 Ω and 8.55 Ω, respectively. The low equivalent series resistance (Rs) is due to the good electrical conductivity of electrode materials. The cycle stability of the electrode was assessed by measuring it at a current density of 20 A g−1. Following 2000 cycles, the electrode composed of SN-GQDs experiences a reduction in its specific capacitance from 118.47 F g−1 to 93.77 F g−1, indicating a cyclic stability of around 80 % retention as shown in Fig. 6f. This outcome demonstrates a commendable level of cyclability.

The electrical conductivity of PANI and SN-GQDs/PANI composite was measured utilizing the four-probe technique [84]. The measurement of resistance was conducted within the range of −1 to 1 V for voltage and 10 mA for current, while the thickness of the sheet was determined using a micrometer (Mitutoyo C/N 293-821-30) with 1 μm resolution. The SN-GQDs/PANI composite shows better electrical conductivity as can be seen in Table 2 . The enhancement in the electrical conductivity will lead to an increase in the specific capacitance or the energy density of the supercapacitor.

The fabrication of symmetric SCs involved the utilization of PANI and a composite material consisting of SN-GQDs/PANI. Fig. 7 and Fig. 8 display the CV, GCD, and EIS measurements of the supercapacitors. The CV curve has a characteristic rectangular form that has a broad hump, while the GCD curve demonstrates a symmetric triangular shape with almost linear straight lines as shown in Fig. 7(a-b). This observation verifies the presence of both electric double-layer capacitor (EDLC) [85] and pseudocapacitive charge storage [86,87] capabilities in the system. The potential window of PANI supercapacitors was found to be 0.8 V [88], but the potential window of the SN-GQDs/PANI composite was measured to be 1 V can be seen in Fig. 7(a-b). This observation indicates that the composite material possesses a higher energy density compared to PANI.

CV was conducted on both PANI and SN-GQDs/PANI composite symmetric supercapacitors, with scan rates ranging from 10 mV/s to 100 mV/s. The specific capacitance was determined using the GCD method from Eq. 2, and the results are presented in Table 3 . Additionally, a comparison of the energy density and power density of the device is provided in Table 4 , surpassing the energy densities of most symmetrical supercapacitors documented in prior studies can be seen in Table 1. The higher specific capacitance value in the SN-GQDs/PANI compared to pristine PANI is because of the better crystalline structure of the composite which is confirmed by the structural analysis (XRD, Raman, and FTIR). However, by addition of SN-GQDs in PANI makes the composite much better electrically conductive material because of its crystalline nature and gives more electrochemical active sites in the composite. These factors combined suggest that SN-GQDs/PANI composites can indeed exhibit superior electrochemical properties compared to pristine PANI. Also, the Ragone plot shows the comparison of the energy density with power density for the PANI and the SN-GQDs/PANI can be seen in Fig. 7g.

Fig. 8(a–b) illustrates the Nyquist plot, where the SN-GQDs/PANI and PANI symmetric supercapacitors demonstrate a distinct semi-circular shape in the high-frequency region, indicating a reduced charge transfer resistance of SN-GQDs/PANI compared to PANI. The Rs and Rct values of the PANI and SN-GQDs/PANI composite are listed in Table 5 . This drop in resistance has a direct effect on diffusion rates, resulting in increased electrode conductivity and storage capacity.

The extended lifespan of the device is a crucial factor for its practical use. Continuous measurement of charge and discharge was conducted at a current density of 20 A g−1 for a total of 5000 cycles as shown in Fig. 8c. The results indicate a significant improvement in capacitance retention, with approximately 74 % retention achieved. This improvement can be attributed to the utilization of SN-GQDs in the synthesis of PANI. In contrast, the cyclability test conducted on PANI alone exhibited poor results, with only around 30 % retention observed after 1000 cycles.

Configurations were employed to connect three supercapacitor units in series to showcase practical applicability. These arrangements facilitate the subsequent activation of a small DC motor, thereby implying that the developed supercapacitor facility possesses practical functionality. Consequently, this facility can be effectively utilized in real-world industrial settings or comparable environments. The obtained results confirm the potential practical application of a distinct material consisting of SN-GQDs.

SN-GQDs were successfully synthesized using the MAH method in a controlled way to get the 4.1 ± 0.7 nm particle size. The electrode prepared using SN-GQDs exhibits the excellent specific capacitance of 1040 F/g at a current density of 0.5 A g−1 in the three-electrode configuration. Also, PANI and SN-GQDs/PANI composite were synthesized and the symmetric SCs were fabricated from these two materials. At a current density of 0.5 A g−1, the symmetric SC fabricated from the PANI shows an energy density of 18.71 Wh/kg at a power density of 800 W/kg. However, SC fabricated using SN-GQDs/PANI composite at the same current density shows an energy density of 44.72 Wh/kg at a power density of 1227 W/kg. Also, the SCs fabricated from SN-GQDs/PANI composite show capacitance retention of 74 % after the 5000 charge/discharge cycle. The enhanced energy density is attributed to the doping of heteroatoms, which improves the electrical conductivity and also acts as a trap site to attract more electrolyte ions. Overall, SN-GQDs display an excellent specific capacitance and enhancement in the energy density in conjunction with PANI showing the materials are suitable for use in energy storage applications.
